title: Roast Goose at Christmas
subtitle: The Victorian centrepiece: a bird that bastes itself and fills the pan with precious fat
excerpt: Goose was the original British Christmas bird before turkey took over in the twentieth century. It is fattier and richer than turkey, produces spectacular crackling-like skin, and yields several jars of fat to use for roast potatoes throughout the year.
sourceNotes: Charles Dickens, A Christmas Carol (1843): the Cratchits' goose as the festive centrepiece. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: roasting goose with apple stuffing. Traditional Victorian British practice.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Season the bird and refrigerate uncovered 24 hours ahead to dry the skin. The prune and apple stuffing can be made the day before and refrigerated.
recipe.temperatureNote: reduce to 180°C after the first 30 minutes

glossaryTerms.definition: The slow melting of fat from meat during cooking. A goose renders an exceptional quantity of fat during roasting; several jars of golden goose fat can be saved afterward and used for months to crisp roast potatoes and sauteed vegetables.

The internal organs of a bird, including the liver, heart, gizzard, and neck, usually packaged inside or alongside the bird. Set aside before roasting; simmer in water for 45 minutes to make a stock for the gravy.

content.text: Goose fell from the British Christmas table largely because it cannot be scaled up for a large family the way a 7 kg turkey can; a 4.5 kg goose feeds six at most. But as a two-family centrepiece or a smaller gathering's showpiece, it remains the superior bird: darker meat throughout, richer flavour, and skin that crisps into something approaching crackling.
The fat is a bonus. A single goose yields 400-600 ml of rendered goose fat, which sets solid in a jar and keeps in the fridge for weeks. Check the cavity before roasting for the
packet. Simmer the neck and gizzard in a litre of water for the gravy stock while the bird roasts.

items.prepNote: goose, approx. 4.5 kg, oven-ready, giblets removed and reserved
for the gravy, or use giblet stock
slaked in cold water, to thicken gravy

content.text: Remove the giblets and any fat pads from inside the cavity; set the neck and gizzard aside for stock. Pat the skin thoroughly dry. Prick the fatty areas of the skin (thighs, lower breast) all over with a skewer to help the fat render during roasting. Rub
over the whole bird. Fill the cavity loosely with the apple quarters, onion, and

Heat the oven to 200°C (180°C fan). Set the bird breast-up on a rack in the roasting pan. Roast for 30 minutes, then reduce to 180°C (160°C fan). Every hour, baste with the pan fat and drain off the rendered fat into a heatproof jug using a ladle or turkey baster. A 4.5 kg bird needs roughly 3 hours total, or until the inner thigh reads 75°C. The
slows considerably in the final hour once most of the fat has left the skin.
Transfer the bird to a warm board and rest for 20 minutes. Strain the fat from the roasting pan into jars and save. In the pan, add
if using, scrape up the dark fond, and add
or giblet stock. Simmer for 5 minutes. If you want a thicker gravy, stir in the cornflour slurry and simmer for 2 minutes. Strain into a warm jug.

rows.symptom: Skin is not as crisp as expected
rows.cause: Too much moisture on the skin surface, or fat was not drained during cooking
rows.fix: Drain fat every hour. If the skin is still soft at the end, increase to 220°C for 15 minutes. Prick the skin more aggressively before roasting next time.

rows.symptom: Breast is drying out before the legs are cooked
rows.cause: Goose breast cooks faster than the legs
rows.fix: Cover the breast loosely with foil from the halfway point and remove it for the last 30 minutes to colour the skin.

rows.symptom: The roasting pan is overflowing with fat
rows.cause: Normal for a large bird; goose yields more fat than any other roasting bird
rows.fix: Drain into a heatproof jug every 45-60 minutes. Have a second large vessel standing by.

rows.cause: The pan fond has not caramelised enough, or the giblet stock was not used
rows.fix: Let the pan sit over high heat for 2 minutes before adding liquid. Giblet stock makes a markedly better gravy than plain chicken stock.

content.text: Roast goose is the original British Christmas dinner. Turkey replaced it gradually through the mid-twentieth century as cheap commercial birds became widely available, but the goose retained its hold in households that prized flavour over economy. The fat, strained and potted, is a bonus that extends the bird's value across the weeks that follow.
